story:
The first time I used the machine, nothing seemed different. I stepped out of the pod after the lights shut down, went home, and slept. In the morning I was three inches taller and almost thirty pounds heavier, all muscle. People stared at work. I had taken the janitor job at Caltech on purpose. I'd always had a knack for math and science, even if I'd been misguided and done forty-six months in Folsom. On parole, you take the work you can get. Caltech felt like fate.

The second time, I didn't just leave the pod. I went somewhere else—hot air, stone temples, and the faint sound of cars. I think it was Cambodia sometime in the last century. I was there a minute or two, then back. The next morning my left ring finger was gone.

The third time I stood beside Romulus and Remus and heard them speak. I don't know Latin, so I couldn't understand, but I knew where I was: ancient Rome. When I returned, I had an identical twin. He couldn't talk, but he understood me. I told him to follow me. I drove him to my place. I told myself two of me couldn't exist here without everything falling apart. I told myself it was survival. Then I shot him and hid what I'd done. I knew it was wrong. I did it anyway.

I timed my cleaning of Lecture Hall 208 to catch Professor Hallison's class. He said they had a breakthrough that would change the world, but he wouldn't give details—non-disclosure agreements and a pending announcement. For a second I imagined hitting him with my mop handle. I didn't. Not then.

The fourth time, I went to a white place with no walls, no ceiling, no floor—just emptiness. I called out and got nothing back. I jogged through nowhere until the pod took me home. When the lights died, my left eye was blind.

A week later, I brought Professor Hallison back to my apartment. I warned him to stay quiet and asked about the machine. He surprised me. "Shooting your twin and hiding him didn't teach you anything?" he said. Then he told me they knew someone had been using it at night. Security was being posted at the lab. I asked him what the machine was. He told me the truth: they didn't know yet. That's why there wasn't an announcement. They knew it did something. They didn't know what. I asked how to undo what it had done to me—my missing finger, my ruined eye. He said it was impossible to say. I heard that phrase twice. I saw no way forward. I killed him. I cleaned up. I hadn't slept in days. My head pounded. My nose bled. None of that excused it.

The fifth time was the last. I got past the guard outside the lab. I told myself I had no choice. I used the machine.

I ended up in a swamp I don't recognize, maybe not even in my time anymore. I think I saw a small dinosaur dart through the trees this morning. I arrived missing my left leg below the knee. I can't walk. I lean against a tree and try to think.

The first trip was pure gain—height and strength. But after that, each trip gave me something and took more away—a finger, then a twin I destroyed myself, then an eye, then a leg. Curiosity carried me this far. They say curiosity killed the cat.

I am the cat.
